text: The surgeon encounters a challenge as the cord lipoma is connected to the retroperitoneal lymph nodes, complicating the dissection in the direct and indirect spaces.
The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap, initiating the dissection medially in the right inguinal region.
The surgeon navigates pre-transversalis fascia, attempting to access the pre-peritoneal space laterally.
The surgeon identifies the pubic bone while examining the inguinal canal, noting the absence of a significant indirect sac and the presence of hidden fat due to the large inguinal hernia.
The surgeon works on parietalizing the cord structures in the inguinal region, with visible challenges in maintaining adequate working space due to the absence of a hernia sac.

The surgeon observes increased respiratory variation in the patient, indicating pressure changes. Despite this, the surgeon continues to sweep cord structures laterally, noting the absence of a hernia sac.
The surgeon continues dissection in the area with current exposure, but the surrounding tissue begins to collapse, reducing the working space.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens in the surgical field but struggles with maintaining exposure. A sponge is introduced to aid in retraction, preventing tearing of the surrounding fat. A large mass of fat is visible to the right, likely contributing to the patient's symptomatic hernia.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia while focusing on visualizing the cord structures, but struggles to maintain a clear view of the surgical field.
The abdomen visibly re-insufflates, improving the surgical field visibility and facilitating the continuation of the hernia procedure.
The surgeon parietalizes the cord structures, identifying the vas deferens in an area without a hernia sac, ensuring no injuries occur during the process.
The surgeon locates the spermatic cord amidst the surrounding tissue, using a sponge for retraction to maintain a clear view.

The surgeon begins reducing a large lipoma, encountering blood vessels that complicate the process. The lipoma is connected to retroperitoneal lymph nodes herniating into the indirect space, requiring careful removal to prevent obstruction of the mesh placement.

The surgeon attempts to resect tissue from the indirect space to prevent future herniation and ensure proper mesh placement against the right MPO. The tissue is mobilized out of the indirect space, revealing its extensive reach.
The surgeon isolates and removes the remaining portion of the lipoma, which is visibly extending and connected to the lateral aspect of the cord.
The surgeon carefully protects the cord structures while identifying additional tissue extending upwards. The tissue is then amputated at the specified level.
The surgeon exposes the remaining retroperitoneal nodes, which could affect the placement of the mesh. A partner is consulted to assist in resecting the nodal tissue.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the tissue and continues to parietalize, establishing the right medial preperitoneal orientation (MPO).
The surgeon identifies a large pocket within the surgical field and prepares to close the flap using a sixteen twelve pro grip mesh, suturing it securely in place.
